meaning: possessive suffix pronoun of the 3rd fem. singular ('her, its') used with a noun ending in a vowel
origin_explanation: it is also used with expressions of time; e.g. sobe-hā 'afterward', 'ame-ha 'then'; SEMITIC: Ar. -hā, SAr. (Sabaean) -h, Heb. -hā in special occurrences, Aram.-Syr. -āh. For this suffix pronoun in the other Semitic languages, see Brockelmann 1908:311-2; Barth 1913:56ff. ETHIOPIC: Te. -ha, Gaf. -hā. For the other Ethio-pian languages, see Leslau 1956:58.
amharic_meaning: የ3ኛ መደብ አንስታይ ነጠላ ባለቤትነት አገናዛቢ ተውላጠ ስም (የእሷ፣ የሱ/ለነገር) በአናባቢ ለሚያልቁ ስሞች

meaning: marker of the accusative with proper nouns (e.g. yəhudā-hā 'Judah' [acc.]).
'there, here, away to' (e.g. kʷ'əlla-he, ba-kʷ'əlla-he 'everywhere', kʷ'əlla-he [acc] 'in every direction').

'also, and, further, even, the very, for one's part' (e.g. 'ana-hi 'I, for my part'); is attached to mannu, mənt, 'ayte (mannu-hi, mənta-hi, 'ayte-hi), mostly in negative sen-tences; it expresses the indefinite 'no one, noth-ing, nowhere'; it is combined with 'amma- in 'amma-hi 'though, even though'; 'amma...wəla'amma-hi 'either...or'; 'amma-hi...wə'amma-hi 'either...or'; in the meaning 'and' it can be repeated, as in 'ana-hi wəwə'ətu-hi 'both I and he, I and he'; or in -hi...-ni, as in 'əmmuntu-hi wə'əbuhomu-ni 'they as well as their father'.
possessive suffix pronoun of the 3rd masc. sg. ('his, its') used with a noun ending in a vowel; occasionally used as the definite article;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. -hu 'his, its', SAr. (Sabaean) -hw, -h, Mh.Soq. -h, -eh, Heb. -hū (occasionally), Aram.-Syr. -h, -eh, Ph.Ug. -h. For more details on this suffix pronoun, see Brockelmann 1908:311-2; Barth 1913:49 ff. ETHIOPIC: Te. -hu, Gaf. -ho; for the contracted form -u in the other Ethiopian languages, see Leslau 1956:58.
amharic_meaning: የ3ኛ መደብ ተባዕታይ ነጠላ ባለቤትነት አገናዛቢ ተውላጠ ስም (የእሱ፣ የሱ/ለነገር) በአናባቢ ለሚያልቁ ስሞች፤ አልፎ አልፎ እንደ መስተአምር ያገለግላል

meaning: it serves as an interrogative particle (e.g. ta'ammənu-hu 'do you believe?'); it expresses doubt or uncertainty (see Dillmann 1907:378, 514); it also serves for the reinforce-ment of a conditional sentence (e.g. 'əmma-ssa 'anta-hu 'if it is indeed thou'); see also Dill-mann 1907:550;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. -hu in the dialects, and occasionally in classical Arabic; see Brockelmann 1913:190, and Landberg 1909:1362 who identifies it with the suffix pronoun of the 3rd masc. singular. ETHIOPIC: also in Cushitic: Sa. -ho, interrogative.
amharic_meaning: እንደ መጠይቃዊ ቅጥያ ያገለግላል፤ ጥርጣሬን ወይም አለመረጋጋትን ይገልጻል

meaning: (masc.sg.), habu (masc.pl.), habā (fem.pl.) 'then!, now then!, well!, come on!';
origin_explanation: from wahaba 'give', below; SEMITIC: Ar. hab, hāb, exclamation (see Schulthess, ZA 19 [1905].131; Noeldeke, ZDMG 66 [1912].736-7), Soq. habu, hibo 'let (one do something!)', Heb. hab, hābā 'go on, now then!'.
SEMITIC: related to Ar. habāba 'dew drops'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hubo 'dew', Gur. ab°ā, awā.

phonetics: hababa I (MA; rather than habba given by MA) 'blow, be windy';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. habba 'blow (wind)'; related to haffa 'blow'. ETHIOPIC: Te. habba (hbb) 'roar (wind)'; Har. ha-bāb bāya 'roar (wind)' also in Cushitic: Bil. habba 'wind'.

tahababa (tahababa) 'defame, reproach, revile, slander, (T.M) disparage, grumble'; also 'be insulted, insult one another'. astahababa, mastahababa (T.M) 'grumble, revile, disparage, insult one another'. habābī, māhbabī, māhbāb (K*), part. of tahabba. mastahabāb, act. part. of astahababa. ETHIOPIC: Amh. habhabāb 'mock, deride'. See also hababa I.
from wahaba 'give', below. SEMITIC: Heb. habhāb-īm interpreted as 'gifts, offerings'; see Buhl 172, Baumgartner 227.

tahablaya 'amuse oneself, procure for oneself a spectacle'. habluy 'spectacle'. hablayā (Lt) 'welcome sight'. SEMITIC: The connection with Heb. habal 'be vain' suggested by Stade 45 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hablaya (with h) 'deceive by a hoax'. See also hawlaya I.
to be connected with harbada, below.
from wahaba 'give', below.

phonetics: hobāy (pl. hobāyāt, wahbūy) 'kind of hawk, kite, (T.M. Sw. in D 9) ape';
meaning: 'kind of hawk, kite, (T.M. Sw. in D 9) ape'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. habāy, humbay 'baboon, monkey', hobāy 'kite', Tna. habāy 'monkey', hobāy samay 'kind of hawk'. Amh. eba 'monkey', monkey's cry', from Cushitic: Sa.Af. habu, habūbā 'ape', habū. The sound of the ape'. There is the possibility of separating hobāy I 'kite' from hobāy II 'monkey'. However, the root hobāy meaning 'monkey' and 'kite' could be explained by a sound that may be considered common to both the monkey and the kite; in this case there would be a single root hobāy.

phonetics: had'a (yahdā') , (L.T) had'a 'be tranquil, be quiet, quiet oneself, be at peace, be appeased, abate, subside';
meaning: 'be tranquil, be quiet, quiet oneself, be at peace, be appeased, abate, subside'
origin_explanation: ahdā'a 'calm, quiet, appease, make pacify, mitigate'. hadā'i (Gr. 13), see hadu. hadū' 'calm, quiet, tranquil, placid, relieved, calmed down'. hadūwāwī 'inclined to quiet, to calm'. had'āt, (T) had'at 'tranquility, serenity, quietness, calm, repose, peace, modesty, bashfulness, gentleness'. (bahad'at 'quietly'). ahdā'i, māhdā'i (Gr), part. of ahdā'a. māhdā'i 'who brings quiet, who tranquilizes, who calms'. SEMITIC: Ar. hada'a 'be quiet', Soq. hyode, Sh. hede. For Heb. hdy, see Greenspahn 85. ETHIOPIC: Te. had'a 'become quiet'. Tna. had'e. Amh. da alā 'be quiet'; also in Cushitic. Be. ad, haduw 'tranquility'. Af. idiyā. See also hdy.
amharic_meaning: መረጋጋት፣ ጸጥ ማለት፣ ሰላም መሆን፣ ማብረድ፣ መቀነስ

origin_explanation: from Amh. addāfā 'be dirty'.

phonetics: haddama (D.Y), haddama 'sleep, slumber, (T.Y) tell idle stories, (K) be dark (night)'; note that KG differentiates between hadama 'slumber' and haddama 'tell stories';
meaning: 'sleep, slumber, (T.Y) tell idle stories, (K) be dark (night)'
origin_explanation: tahaddama, (L) tahadma, (T) tahadma 'come drowsy, be overcome by sleep, be created, be invented' (connected with the meaning 'phantom', below). hadmat, (T) hadmat 'deep sleep, numbness, torpor, absurdity'. mahaddamat, māh(a)damat 'dreaminess, reverie, phantom stories, fable, frivolity, absurdities, insani. ty, (Lt) imagination, hallucination, drolleries' (nagara mahaddamt 'fictitious story'); mohdəm (M), mohdām (T) 'place where one sleeps, place where one stretches out to to sleep, rug, carpet, mat'. it is doubtful whether the various meanings belong to the same root. SEMITIC: Ar. hudūm 'giddiness'. Hurwitz 95 identifies it with the root dmm, dwm. Heb. dāmām 'silence', dūmā 'silence'. Bittner, WZKM 30 (1917-8), 425 connects it with Soq. hidem-an 'dream', šodim 'to dream', back to dmy. Heb. dāmā (dmy) 'take a rest'. Praeto-rius, ZDMG 57 (1903), 271 connects it with hrm (with alternance r:d), but hrm expresses kind of misfortune and does not agree in meaning with G. hdm.
ETHIOPIC: Te. hadma 'escape, run away'. Tna. ma.

phonetics: hadama II (Y), hadama 'delimit, establish, determine, fix';
origin_explanation: tahadama (Lt), passive. ETHIOPIC: Tna. haddāmā 'build an enclosure'. Addāmā 'build a fence or enclosure of stone', 'fence of stone constructed around the city'.

phonetics: hadmat (Lt) 'a garment of several pieces sewn together';
meaning: 'a garment of several pieces sewn together'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. hidm 'worn-out garment'. (ta)haddama 'be worn-out (garment)'. ETHIOPIC: Te. hadāmāt 'shabby clothes'.
related to had'a, above.

phonetics: hadaya, hadaya (T.Y.K*.M) 'dissolve by overcooking, cook completely';
origin_explanation: tahadya, (Lt) tahadya 'be burned through, be scorched, be dissolved by being cooked too much, be macerated, be fully cooked, be sepa-rated, (K*) become soft, (Lt) dry up'. SEMITIC: related to Ar. hdy, ahdā 'boil meat to shreds' (Dillmann 18). Fisher 73 compares Ug. hdy 'lacerate'.

phonetics: hafawa (T) hafawa 'be hot, get heated, sweat, perspire';
origin_explanation: hafāwī (Gr.15) 'who perspires'. hafāw (Gr.15) 'full of perspiration'. haf, hāf, haf 'heat, sweat, (Gr) dampness, moisture'. hafwat (Gr.15) 'act of perspiring'. SEMITIC: related to Ar. hawf, hayf 'hot wind'. ETHIOPIC: Te. haf 'heat', Tna. hafa 'fever'.

phonetics: hag 'la (yahg'al) , (L) hag'la 'be lost, be destroyed, perish, be deprived of';
meaning: 'be lost, be destroyed, perish, be deprived of'
origin_explanation: hag'alā (yahg'al) 'lose, suffer a loss, destroy'. ahg'alā 'destroy, lose, make lose, waste, ruin, defraud, put off'. tahag'la, tahag'la (T) tahag'la 'perish, be destroyed, be ruined, be lost, fail, die'. tahāg'alā (M) 'destroy one another, ruin one another'. vastahag'alā (DTW), caus. of tahāg'alā. hag'al (hagul) 'lost, who loses, destroyed, ruined, debased, decaying, doomed to destruction, rejected, pernicious'. hag'l 'destruction, perdition, loss, waste, corruption, perfidy, havoc, ruin, end; place of perdition' (that is, abode of the dead). hag'lat 'loss, perdition'. yahg'ālī, act. part. of ahg'alā. māhg'al (māhgūl) 'destroying, deceitful, destructive, pernicious'. māh(a)g'ālī 'destroyer, who causes ruin, pernicious, who corrupts, destructive'. mohg'ālī (Lt) 'place of destruction'. tahag'ālī, part. of tahag'alā. tahag'lo (t) 'ruination, extermination, destruction'. tahg'ālī, same. SEMITIC: Ar. hgl, ahgala 'lose (wealth)'. The connection with Ar. halaka 'perish, die' suggested by Dillmann 18 is not convincing. ETHIOPIC: Te. hagla 'vanish, perish', Tna. hag'alā 'abandon, impoverish'. Amh. agg'alā 'put off'; also in Cushitic. Kham. ak'el 'disappear'. See also hagala.

meaning: 'city, town, village, province, district, country, homeland, inhabited region';'(hagar taqm 'walled city')';'(hagara saqam 'walled city')';'(hagar ṣanāt 'strong city')';'(saba hagar 'citizens')';'(ra'sa hagar 'capital')';'hagarit, (T) hagarīt 'town, city, state, towns-people, citizens, intimate(s), friends, acquaint-ance(s), (K*) uneducated'';'hagarāwī 'urban, pertaining to a city, civic, citizen'';'hagarītāwī, (Lt) hagarītāy 'citizen, townsman, (K*) pagan'';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) hagar 'village in ruins', (Yem) hiġreh 'protected enclave', SAr. hgr 'city'. ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. hagar 'land, country', Amh. Gur. agār. See on this root Cohen 1947:no. 1.
amharic_meaning: ከተማ፣ መንደር፣ ግዛት፣ አውራጃ፣ ሀገር፣ የትውልድ ሀገር፣ ሰው የሚኖርበት አካባቢ

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. haggära 'dye red'.

meaning: 'be eager, be intent on something, be diligent, hurry, be fast';hagrut, (T) hagruī 'intent on something, atten-tive, eager, solicitous, who applies oneself, anxious';hagratannā, (T) hagratannā 'assiduity, applying oneself, anxiety, solicitude, (T) speed';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Hommel 1877:56, n. 54 considers it a denominative of hagarit (see hagar) with the mean-ing 'in der Politik nach etwas streben'. This connec-tion is doubtful. See also (ta)agrata, gāhrata.
amharic_meaning: መጓጓት፣ በአንድ ነገር ላይ ማተኮር፣ ትጉህ መሆን፣ መቸኮል፣ መፍጠን

origin_explanation: from Amh. agg"āt; also in Tna. angat (recorded by Reinisch 1890:189); perhaps from Cushitic: Sa. han-gāt 'buttermilk'.

meaning: given by MA 232) 'produce light, make pro-duce light';
origin_explanation: a misprint in relation to hanwaza, below.

meaning: 'letter of the alphabet, (Lt) one iota';hohayāta fidal, (Lt) hohayāta fidal 'letters';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. hahu alā 'learn the alphabet, 'syllabary symbol, letter', Te. hahuhi be 'read the alphabet', from the first letter of the alphabet, read ha, hu, hi, and so on.

meaning: hakaka (TS) 'quarrel, cause insurrection, agitate, per-turb, trouble';ahkaka (Gr.6), causative;tahakaka (K) 'be tumultuous';tahakaka (Gr.6) 'cause to be tumultuous';hakakī (Gr.6) 'tumultuous';hakuk 'turbulent, seditious, perturb-ed, troubled';hakak 'tumult, riot, revolt, turmoil, terror, alarm, uproar, quarrel, dissension, consternation, commotion, chaos, disorder, (Gr.6) religious persecution';hakak (T), same;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: related to hwk, hoka.
amharic_meaning: መጣላት፣ አመጽ ማስነሳት፣ ማነሳሳት፣ ማወክ፣ ማስቸገር

meaning: hakaya 'be lazy, be slug-gish, be slack, be inactive, be weary, lan-guish, be indolent';ahkaya, causative;tahakaya, (Gr.7) tahakkaya 'be sluggish, be slack, be slothful, be weary, be nonchalant, neglect, be negligent, be lazy, be indolent, be inert, be tranquil, be idle, be remiss, cease, hes-itate, delay, decline, disdain';astahakaya, caus. of tahakaya; also (Gr.7) 'de-spise, disdain, bring about dissension';hakkāy 'sluggard, indolent, inert, idle, lan-guishing, lazy, slothful, negligent';hakāyī 'weariness, idleness, slothfulness, lazi-ness, apathy, inertia, negligence';tahakāyī, part. of tahakaya;mahakāy 'one who causes weariness, wearisome';mastahakāy (Gr.7, K*), part. of astahakaya;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. kahiya (with metathesis) 'be weak, cowardly'. Mh. hatki (from hky) 'bend down'. Heb. kāhā (khy) 'grow dim, grow dull'. Syr. qāhā (qā) 'blunt, be dull' (so also Brockelmann 1928:650). Note that Dillmann 10 compares G. hakaya to these roots, but with reservations. Holma, ZA 28 (1914).147 identifies it with Akk. akū 'destitute, weak' (and other related Semitic roots), but von Soden 30 considers akū 'cripple' a Sumerian loan-word. ETHIOPIC: Te. hakka (hky) 'be weary, be indolent'. Tna. hakayā. Amh. haket 'indolence' (from Ge'ez), and a denominative takkātā (from t-akkātā) 'be tired, become weary'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. hakay 'tired, weary'. Qem. akēt 'laziness'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. hela 'cistern, deep well'; variant of (ela).
probably transcription of Heb. hol 'profane'.
ETHIOPIC: It is tempting to connect it with Sa. halkas 'cause discord'; the whirlwind would be something that causes atmospheric disturbance.

SEMITIC: Ar. hallala 'rejoice, cheer', Heb. hillel 'praise, intone the Hallelujah', Aram. hallel 'praise', Syr. hellel 'rejoice', Md. hll 'utter cries of praise', Ug. hll 'shouting', Akk. alālu, atlulu 'shout' (an exclamation of joy)'.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. halali, halāla 'donkey that bray's when at rut', Te. halla 'cover (animal)'. Amh. allalā 'be in heat', alāle 'of dissolute life', Gur. alāle 'donkey that is used for sexual intercourse'; also in Cushitic: Or. alali. See also allala.
from Ar. hilāl 'new moon'; also in Amh. halāl 'full moon'.

meaning: 'hallelujah';hälletā 'the chanting of hallelujah', (Gr.2) col-lection of various modes of singing halle-lujah';('anqaṣa hälletā 'index of type of melodies');
origin_explanation: from Heb. hal(l)əlū yāh 'praise God!' through Gk. allēlouia; also Tna. halleluya. See also hll.

meaning: helänsän (K*) 'turban, referring to wrapping, (KG) carpet, rug';
origin_explanation: perhaps from Gk. heliksin, acc. sg. of heliksis 'rolled bandage'. The difference (k:n) could be explained by confusing n and h k.

meaning: 'be, exist, be present, be extant, there is, be available, abide, live';also as present 'is';the verb hallawa, hallo + imperfect of another verb (or imperfect + hallawa, hallo) expresses duration in the past or in the future;

the verb hallawa with object suffix pronouns followed by a jussive expresses an obligation or an instant future (e.g. hallawakkəmu tərwaya-wo 'you will be obliged to see him'); for more details, see Cohen 1924:94-6; (za-hallo 'existing'); (za-hallo 'non-existing'); hallawa 'give existence, make live'; hallawi 'essential, substantial, unfailing, who exists, who lives'; hallaw (fem. hallut) 'existing, who exists, who lives, that lasts, lasting, constant, that is pres-ent, who originates, that is true, that is exact'; (ba-hallaw, hallawa 'really, exactly, truly'); halläwe 'substance, essence, nature, existence, hypostasis, being, state, presence, position, condition, (Lt) dwelling, residence'; (i-halläwe 'absence'); halläwayät (pl. of halläwe) 'elements'; halläwo(t), see halläwe; also 'vicissitude, chance'; (i-halläwo, i-hallo 'nothingness'); hallunä 'essence'; məhallaw 'dwelling place, domicile';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Yemen) hallo 'there is' (W. Müller, ZDMG 131 [1981].399). Dillmann 3 connects it with Ar. hāl 'state'. Praetorius 1890:34 identifies it with Ar. halala which he translates 'wait and hesitate' according to the Qāmūs, and with hallala 'remain behind'. This identification is doubtful. Cohen 1924: 94 quotes Som. hay, al 'be' according to Reinisch. Cohen 1947:no.27 connects hallawa with Ar. (Ta'izz) ma 'allōš 'there is not', but see Rodinson, JA 238 (1950).160 who analyzes ma 'allōš as coming from ma lahu šay' and does not see a connection with hallawa. ETHIOPIC: Te. halla 'be', Tna. 'allo, Arg. halla, Har. hal, Amh. allä, Gur. alä. For more details, see Leslau 1956:79-80. For G. halläwe 'hypostasis', see Cerulli 1957:20-23. For the combination of hallawa with various tenses, see Cohen 1924:94-6, 198-9, 254.
amharic_meaning: ግዴታን ወይም የቅርብ ጊዜን ለማመልከት ያገለግላል

origin_explanation: perhaps from Gk. hēlios 'sun' combined with the letter alef; or could it be identified with həlāl 'new moon'?

meaning: -homu, suffix pronoun of the 3rd masc. pl. attached to nouns ending in a vowel;
amharic_meaning: የ3ኛ መደብ ተባዕታይ ብዙ ቁጥር አገናዛቢ ተውላጠ ስም (የእነሱ) በአናባቢ ለሚያልቁ ስሞች

origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. 'omer. See also gomor.

meaning: -hon, suffix pronoun of the 3rd fem. pl. attached to nouns ending in a vowel;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. -hunnā, SAr. (Sabaean) -hn, Heb. -hen after a vowel, Aram. -hōn, Ug. -hn. For more details, see Brockelmann 1908:312-3; Barth 1913:66ff. ETHIOPIC: Te. -han.
amharic_meaning: የ3ኛ መደብ አንስታይ ብዙ ቁጥር አገናዛቢ ተውላጠ ስም (የእነሱ) በአናባቢ ለሚያልቁ ስሞች

origin_explanation: see also henäli.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. hanka 'take!', Te.Tna. 'anka 'take!, Amh. 'ənka 'take!, have!, here!'. See also ka III, and 'ənka.

meaning: (KBT.M), hanos (T) 'sphere above the sky which is one-third water, (KG) turbid water', (L) proper name of an ocean
origin_explanation: in view of the variant haros it is perhaps derived from Gk. ouranos 'sky'; also in Amh. hanos (from Geez).
amharic_meaning: ከሰማይ በላይ ያለ የውሃ ክምችት፣ የውቅያኖስ ስም

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. ənsät 'Ensete muse'; also ənsät (for which see Strelcyn 1973:no.90).
is a misprint in relation to hagwaza , or the other way around.
happäli (K*) 'one who washes clothes', mahappäl, mahappel, mahappil 'one who washes clothes, fuller';
SEMITIC: Ar. wabala 'hit with a stick', wabäl 'stick with which one beats clothes that are being washed to remove the dirt' (Dillmann 22).
from Gk. hypodiakon.

meaning: hippoditen, heppoditen, həppodite (T) həppodite, heppoditen 'vestment of priest';
origin_explanation: from Gk. hypodytēs 'undergarment'.
ethiopic_word: ሂጶዲጤን፣ ሄጶዲጤን፣ ህጶዲጤ፣ ሕጶዴጤ፣ ሔጶዴጤን፣ ሄጶዲጤ

origin_explanation: see hyp II.
from Gk. epops, acc.sg. epopa 'hoopoe'. See also 'eppopä, heppan. Note that the second p is probably also geminated.
any connection with heppo ?

meaning: (T.Y) harbada 'be vio-lently mad, be maddened, be furious, tremble with fear, (K) be spoiled, behave in a fashion not in keeping with one's true condition, (L) 'wallow in mire';
origin_explanation: taharbada 'be driven by madness'; harbud (L) 'enraged, full of wrath'; harbadənnä (K) 'arrogance, state of being pam-pered'; taharbədo 'violent madness'; SEMITIC: related to Ar. 'arbada 'be quarrelsome, be ill-natured'. ETHIOPIC: Te. harbäda 'stupidity', Tna. 'arbäd-bädä 'gesticulate wildly', 'əbädəbäd bälä, same. Amh. 'aräbädbädä 'be agitated'; enlarged with r in relation to 'əbäda 'be insane, be enraged', above; see also Rū-žička 124. For the initial ' of Ar. and Tna., cp. Tna. 'abädä 'go mad'.
see 'arodayon.
seems to be a reconstructed form of mähraka, for which see below.

probably to be identified with harmaz, below. In this case harmäs would be reconstructed from a pl. *harmäst < harmäzət, by assimilation. Note that h of harmäs could be a variant of harmaz. As for Ar. hirmīs 'lion, rhinoceros, buffalo', it is puzzling in relation to G. harmäs. See also Noeldeke 1910:58.
see hanos.
perhaps corrupt from Gk. hystriks 'porcupine' (Hommel 1877:xxiii).
transcription of Heb. hōšīa, hōšī 'help!'.

(zahosä'nä, ba'äla hosä'nä, 'alata hosä'nä 'Palm Sunday'); (mahätowa hosä'nä 'eve of Palm Sunday'); see hatawa; From Heb. hōšī-'nā 'help, please!' through Gk. hōsanna; also in Te. hosa'ana, 'awsa'ana, Tna. hosa-'na; in Cushitic: Kham. ausana.
undoubtedly a misprint in relation to harapi with the same meaning.

meaning: hasop, hašopu, haspu, hušpu, husopu, hosopu 'hyssop';
origin_explanation: (hamla haspu 'hyssop'); from Gk. hyssōpos 'hyssop'; also in Te. hisop. See also 'azäb.
ethiopic_word: ህሶጵ፣ ህሥጱ፣ ህስጱ፣ ሁስጱ፣ ሁሶጱ፣ ሆስጱ

meaning: (T.M) 'plow, cultivate the land' (the form is strange);
origin_explanation: hesəqäpe (T.M) 'cultivated land, plantation';
from Ar. hawā; also Amh. hawā, Har. hawā, Tna. hawāwā 'air'.

meaning: 'stir up, move, disturb, agitate, excite, toss about, perturb, unsettle, trouble, vex, molest'; 'ahoka (J), causative; tahawaka, tahawaka, tahoka, passive; also 'move about, quake, rebel'; hawāki, act. part. of hoka; also 'inconstant, who causes unrest, agitator'; həwwuk, həwwuk, pass. part. of hoka; huk same as hukat; hukat 'movement, motion, trouble, turmoil, disturbance, tumult, perturbation, commotion, uproar, turbulence, agitation, disorder, riot'; tahawāki 'perturbed, moved violently'; tahawāko 'tumult, disorder, perturbation, unrest, agitation'; mahawāk, māhəwāk, act. part. of hoka;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (ta)hawwaka 'be stupefied, be astounded' (Armbruster 14). The connection with Biblical Aram. hāk in yəhāk 'he goes' suggested by Noel-deke, ZA 20 (1907).142, n.1 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te. hawwāka 'confound, excite', Tna. hawwākā 'agitate, excite, stir up', Amh. awwākā; passed into Cushitic: Bil. hawāk 'excite, stir up', Sa. hawk-e 'tumult'.

meaning: ridicule, mock, make fun of, jeer at, (T.M) despise, (Lt) underestimate, (T) be distrustful'; tahawlaya 'amuse oneself, procure for oneself a spectacle'; hawlayo (Lt) 'spectacle';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Could be considered a variant of hablaya (above), with weakening of b, but this root could also be considered related to Heb. hll (polel) 'make a fool of', Syr. hll (afel) 'deride, mock'. ETHIOPIC: Te. halāwlaw 'fool'; see hablaya.

meaning: mix, mingle, confuse'; see hawala I.

origin_explanation: W. Müller suggests Gk. hýlē 'matter', perhaps rather metathesis of hallāwe (see hallawa).

meaning: 'there, thither, in that place'; bahayya 'there'; əmhayya 'from there, thence'; ənta hayya 'that way, through it'; haba hayya 'thither, to that place';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Barth 1907:20.25 connects it with PbH. hē 'behold!, here is!'. Philippi, ZDMG 29 (1875).172 analyzes hayya and zaya as coming from hā, and za with -(y)ya 'a pronominal root' which he sees in Ar. hiya 'she'. For the final -(y)ya, see zaya, below.
from Amh. ayb.

meaning: 'rob, take by force, plunder, spoil, break (the law), carry away by force, wrest, snatch away, confiscate, seize, wrong, outrage, (figurative) ravish, be witch' (for the figurative meaning 'ravish, be witch', cp. Amh. marrākā 'take prisoner in war, capture, captivate, fascinate'); hayyada, intensive of the preceding meaning 'aheda (Gr.12), causative of heda; tahayyada, passive; hayyādi, act. part. of hayyada; also 'rapacious'; hayyud (Gr.12) 'robbed'; hayyād (Lt) 'extortioner, robber'; hayd 'plunder, pillage, robbery, wresting'; hidat 'plundering, robbery, abduction'; tahayyādi (Gr.12) 'who was robbed'; təhayyədənna 'the condition of being robbed' (təhəddənna of M 283 is perhaps a misprint for təhayyədənna); məhayyəd 'robber, extortioner';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: comes close to the meaning of Ar. hāda (hyd) 'distress, oppress'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. haydādā 'prevent, interdict', hedā 'be taken away (object)'.

meaning: 'guide of the blind', perhaps hayyādō 'making go, walk' hayyāda 'make go';
origin_explanation: from Amh. hedā 'go, walk'; it could also be in connection with Ar. hadā (hdy) 'guide'.
from Ar. haykal, Syr. haykalā, Aram. hēkālā (hēkal 274), also Heb. hēkāl, Ug. hkl < Akk. ekallu. Sumerian origin. For a bibliography, see D. Cohen 1970:18; add Blau 50, n.14, Kaufman 27.
Getatchew, BSOAS 43 (1980). 225, n. 127 connects it with Ar. haykal 'frame'; also Te. Tna. haykāl.
from Gk. hyakinthos 'jacinth'. See also yākānt.

SEMITIC: Ar. 'ayyal 'mountain goat, stag', Šh. hayyol, Sar. 'yl, Heb. 'ayyāl, Aram. 'ayyālā, 'ayyālā, Md. aiāla 'deer', Ug. 'yl, Akk. ayyālu. For a bibliography, see D. Cohen 1970:17. ETHIOPIC: Tna. hay(y)al 'antelope', Amh. hayyal (from Geez).

meaning: 'be a believer, be faithful (in the religious sense), have the faith' (denominative); tahaymana (Gr.11), causative; tahaymana (Gr.11) 'believe, show oneself a believer'; hāymānot, haymānot 'belief, creed, faith, religion'; (hāymānot rətu't 'orthodox religion'); (hāymānot masihāwit 'faith of the Unitedists'; see Crummey 20-3); (hāymānotəna 'abaw 'the Faith of the Fathers', a collection of confessions and testimonies of the Church Fathers concerning their Christian belief; see Hammerschmidt 1977:82); (haddisa hāymānot 'heresy'); (rətu'a hāymānot 'orthodox'); (mazgaba hāymānot 'Treasure of the Creed', see Hammerschmidt 1977:62); hāymānotāwi (Gr.11) 'believer, faithful, religious'; məhayyam 'believer, faithful (in the religious sense), trusty, trustworthy, (Lt) coreligionist';
origin_explanation: for the Aramaic or Syriac origin of hāymānot, see Polotsky 6-7 and others quoted in Polotsky's article, Noeldeke 1910:35; also in Tna.Amh.Gur. haymanot. For G. məhayyam, cp. Amh. māhayyaman, məhayyam, mayyam (from Geez), Gur. mayam (from Amharic).

meaning: 'instead of, in place of, in return for, in substitution for, by reason of, (Gr.8) because of, in compensation, like'; the suffix pronouns are attached to the base hayyante-; e.g. hayyantehu lakrəstos 'the vicar of Christ';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. hayyānta 'replacement for, in place of'. Praetorius 1871:237 identifies it with Tna. kəndi 'instead of', with ni (of Geez) > nd, by assimilation (see also Brockelmann 1908:153), but the Geez h becoming k in Tigrinya is difficult to explain. Rundgren 1955:232 analyzes it either as hayya-n-ta or hayya-anta. Praetorius 1879:270 derives it from G. wahaya 'wander about' > hay(y)at, 'etwa 'Gang. Weg' (vgl. 'Wegen')' > hay(y)anta, with inserted n. However, the verb is wahaya, not wahaya. See also hayyata.
amharic_meaning: በ... ፋንታ፣ በ... ምትክ፣ በ... ምክንያት

meaning: 'strike, smite, shoot with an arrow, throw, hit with a bow and arrow, brandish a spear, pierce, split (someone's head), (L) hit the target by throwing a stone or by shooting an arrow'; ahepa (Gr.13), causative; tahaypa, passive; hayāpi (Gr.13), act. part. of hepa; hayyup (Gr.13), passive part. of hepa; hepu (T) 'disease' (from hepa 'strike', cp. Amh. mātta 'beat', māčč 'disease'); hipat, verbal noun of hepa; tahayāpi (Gr.13) 'struck'; hayp (Gr.13) 'noun'; hayp 'smite, wound'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 16 compares Ar. habba 'cut, sever (sword)'.
(cp. English 'shoot [arrows, plants])'
it is difficult to decide whether hayyata is the original form developed into hayyanta (with an augmented n) or whether the original form is hayyanta becoming hayyata.

meaning: 'be free from care, be careless, be heedless, be unmindful, disregard, overlook, forsake, be indifferent, be neglectful, esteem lightly, disdain, scorn, despise'
'cause to disregard, cause to overlook, cause to be dumbfounded'
amharic_meaning: ቸል እንዲባል ማድረግ፣ ዝም እንዲባል ማድረግ

meaning: 'who neglects, who is heedless, who holds in light esteem'
